slides.title: DevOps & CI/CD Masterclass
slides.subtitle:
Accelerating Software Delivery from Commit to Production
A Practical Guide to Automation, Pipelines, Security, and Deployment Strategies

slides.name: Aravind Govindhasamy
slides.title: Software Developer & IoT Enthusiast
slides.oss_experience: DevOps Automation, CI/CD Pipeline Architect & Infrastructure Engineering Specialist

slides.title: DevOps Philosophy
slides.subtitle: Breaking Down Silos and Embracing the Continuous Delivery Culture

slides.title: What is DevOps?
slides.imageAlt: Dev and Ops teams collaborating around a board
slides.caption: DevOps: The union of people, process, and products
slides.content:
♾️ Culture, Automation, and Trust

DevOps is a set of practices, tools, and a cultural philosophy that automates and integrates the processes between software development and IT teams.

  • 🤝 Culture Shift - Removing silos between Developers (Dev) and Operations (Ops).
  • ⚡ Continuous Feedback - Rapid feedback loops to ensure code reliability and user satisfaction.
  • 🚀 Faster Delivery - Releasing features to production quickly, securely, and repeatedly.

slides.title: Traditional IT vs. DevOps Culture
leftPoints: 🧱 Development focuses on writing code; Operations focuses on system stability.
🐌 Manual deployments scheduled once every few weeks or months.
🗣️ Blame culture during production outages (Dev vs. Ops).
📝 Long, manual regression testing phases before release.
rightPoints: 🤝 Shared ownership: Devs monitor production; Ops write automation code.
⚡ Automated pipelines deploy code multiple times per day.
🔍 Blameless post-mortems focused on systemic fixes, not individual errors.
🚀 Continuous automated testing run on every code commit.

note.text: DevOps bridges the conflicting goals of Dev (maximize velocity/change) and Ops (maximize stability/no change).

box.title: 🛡️ The 5 Pillars of Successful DevOps
list: Culture: Shared responsibility, trust, and open collaboration across all engineering roles.
Automation: Automate everything possible—builds, testing, infrastructure, and releases.
Lean: Eliminate waste, work in small batches, and optimize the value stream map.
Measurement: Collect data on metrics like deployment frequency and mean time to recovery (MTTR).
Sharing: Share knowledge, lessons learned, and tools openly across the organization.

slides.title: The DevOps Lifecycle
slides.subtitle: Understanding the Infinite Continuous Feedback Loop

note.text: The infinity loop represents the continuous, iterative nature of software deployment and operation.

box.title: 📢 Tightening the Loop for Faster Adjustments
list: Plan & Code: Developers track features in agile boards and write code in localized git branches.
Build & Test: The CI pipeline triggers automatically to compile, run tests, and report errors.
Deploy & Operate: Releases are automated; configurations are managed declaratively using orchestration.
Monitor & Feedback: Errors and logs feed directly back to planners to build better features and fixes.

slides.title: Shift-Left Principles
slides.imageAlt: Graph showing cost vs stage of fixing bugs
slides.caption: Catching issues early reduces cost exponentially
slides.content:
🛠️ Shifting Security and Testing Left

Shift-Left refers to moving testing, quality, and security checks earlier in the software development lifecycle.

  • 🔍 Early Defect Detection - Run tests on developers' machines and pull requests.
  • 🛡️ Integrate Security Early - Scan dependencies during the commit stage, not in staging.
  • 💰 Lower Costs - Fixing bugs in code takes minutes; fixing bugs in production takes weeks.

slides.title: Continuous Integration (CI)
slides.subtitle: The Foundation of Automating Builds, Linting, and Code Checks

slides.title: The Core Pillars of Continuous Integration

box.title: 🏗️ Maintaining a Healthy Main Branch
list: Single Source Repository: All code sits in Git; branches are short-lived and merged to a main trunk.
Automated Build: The pipeline compiles/transpiles and checks dependencies on every commit.
Self-Testing Build: Automated unit tests run as part of the build process; failing tests block merges.
Immediate Feedback: Success or failure notifications are sent to developers immediately via slack, mail, or IDE.

slides.title: Automating Code Quality Checks
slides.caption: Automatic checking before code review
slides.content:
🚨 Code Analysis Pipelines

Manual code reviews are slow. Automated checks enforce guidelines before reviewers read a single line of code.

  • 📏 Linting - Formats code and flags common syntactic styling mistakes.
  • 🔬 Static Application Security Testing (SAST) - Analyzes source code for security vulnerabilities.
  • 📊 Code Coverage - Checks how much of the codebase is covered by unit tests (e.g. minimum 80%).

slides.title: Git Branching: GitFlow vs. Trunk-Based
leftPoints: Long-lived branches (develop, release, master/main).
Large, infrequent merges lead to merge conflicts ('merge hell').
High overhead in managing features, releases, and hotfixes.
Slower release cycle: updates wait for release windows.
rightPoints: Developers commit directly or via short PRs to the main branch.
Daily integrations avoid conflicts and simplify branching.
Uses 'feature flags' to hide incomplete features in production.
Enables continuous deployment pipelines.

note.text: DevOps organizations prefer Trunk-Based Development because it keeps integration friction minimal.

slides.title: Continuous Delivery vs. Deployment
slides.subtitle: Understanding the Critical Difference at the Release Gate

slides.title: Continuous Delivery vs. Continuous Deployment
leftPoints: Code is always in a releasable state.
Builds, tests, and packages artifacts automatically.
Requires a manual approval click to push to production.
Ideal for regulated industries requiring formal release approval.
rightPoints: Automates the entire process from commit to production.
Builds, tests, packages, and deploys without human intervention.
Requires zero manual approvals.
Relies on robust test suites and automatic rollbacks to protect users.

note.text: Continuous Delivery prepares the build; Continuous Deployment ships it automatically.
The main differentiator is the presence of a manual release gate in Continuous Delivery.

box.title: 🧱 Foundational Requirements before Automating Production Releases
list: High Automated Test Coverage: Comprehensive unit, integration, and E2E tests are mandatory.
Zero-Downtime Deployments: Infrastructure must support blue-green or rolling deploys.
Automated Rollback Systems: Pipelines must monitor error rates and auto-revert buggy releases.
Feature Flags: Decouple code deployment from feature release, enabling fast trunk commits.

slides.title: CI/CD Pipeline Anatomy
slides.subtitle: Understanding Stages, Triggers, Runners, and Artifacts

note.text: A pipeline consists of distinct, isolated stages. If any stage fails, the entire pipeline run halts, blocking buggy code.

box.title: 📦 Optimizing Pipeline Speeds
list: Dependency Caching: Cache folders like node_modules or .m2 to avoid fetching packages on every run.
Build Artifacts: Output binaries or bundles passed from one stage to another.
Container Images: Building static Docker images ensures that identical runtimes are deployed to staging and production.
Artifact Registries: Stores packaged software securely (e.g. Nexus, Artifactory, GitHub Packages).

slides.title: Runners and Execution Environments
slides.imageAlt: Server rack blades representing cloud runner infrastructure
slides.caption: The execution engines of CI/CD pipelines
slides.content:
⚙️ Hosting Pipeline Runs

A Runner is a machine (virtual or physical) that executes the steps declared in your pipeline scripts.

  • 🌐 Hosted Runners - Managed by cloud providers (e.g. GitHub-hosted runners). Convenient but costly.
  • 🏠 Self-Hosted Runners - Set up on your own infrastructure. Offers complete control over memory, speed, and network.
  • 🐳 Containerized Run environments - Run jobs inside isolated Docker containers for clean, predictable run states.

slides.title: CI/CD Tooling Ecosystem
slides.subtitle: Comparing Top Continuous Integration and Delivery Platforms

slides.title: GitHub Actions vs. Jenkins
leftPoints: Self-hosted, open-source automation server.
Requires dedicated VM maintenance, updates, and configuration.
Highly extensible with thousands of community plugins.
Pipeline configurations defined using Groovy (Jenkinsfile).
rightPoints: Fully managed, hosted directly inside GitHub repositories.
Zero maintenance: runners spin up dynamically on demand.
Ecosystem of pre-built marketplace actions simplifies setup.
Declarative configuration written in clean YAML syntax.

note.text: Jenkins is highly customizable for on-prem networks; GitHub Actions provides instant cloud integration.

box.title: ⚙️ Alternative Enterprise CI/CD Systems
list: GitLab CI/CD: Excellent, unified platform combining Git, CI/CD, security scans, and container registries.
CircleCI: Fast, cloud-native CI platform known for performance, caching speeds, and docker integration.
ArgoCD: Specialized Kubernetes GitOps continuous delivery tool that continuously syncs git changes to live clusters.
Tekton: Kubernetes-native open-source framework for creating serverless CI/CD pipelines.

slides.title: GitHub Actions Deep Dive
slides.subtitle: Writing Declarative Pipelines with Workflows, Jobs, and Actions

slides.title: GitHub Actions YAML Anatomy
slides.imageAlt: Clean code editor displaying YAML properties
slides.caption: YAML-based pipeline configuration
slides.content:
✍️ Workflow YAML Structure

name: Node CI
on:
  push:
    branches: [ main ]
jobs:
  build-and-test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm ci
      - name: Run tests
        run: npm test

slides.title: Workflows, Jobs, and Steps
leftPoints: 💼 Jobs: A collection of steps executed on the same runner.
⚡ Jobs run in parallel by default, but can be configured to depend on each other (needs keyword).
🖥️ runs-on: Defines target operating system (Ubuntu, Windows, macOS).
rightPoints: 👣 Steps: Individual tasks executed sequentially inside a job.
⚙️ run: Executes raw shell commands on the runner system.
🧩 uses: Imports a reusable marketplace Action (e.g. actions/checkout).

note.text: Steps share the same filesystem runner instance, allowing inputs/outputs and binaries to pass from checkouts to builds.

box.title: 🔒 Securing Credentials & API Keys
list: Encrypted Secrets: Store API tokens, passwords, and SSH keys in Settings -> Secrets. Secrets are masked in console output.
Referencing Secrets: Access secrets securely using template syntax: ${{ secrets.AWS_ACCESS_KEY }}.
Environments: Declare specific deployment targets (Staging, Production) with custom approval gates.
OpenID Connect (OIDC): Authenticate dynamically with cloud providers (AWS, GCP, Azure) without storing long-lived credentials.

slides.title: DevSecOps (Pipeline Security)
slides.subtitle: Embedding Security Checks Directly Into The CI/CD Automation Flow

slides.title: Why DevSecOps?
slides.caption: Security is a continuous practice, not a release checkbox
slides.content:
🛡️ Integrating Security Checks

Traditional security reviews are post-build, creating major release blocks. DevSecOps embeds automated security checks in the developer's daily pipeline.

  • 🛠️ Automated Scans - Dependency, secret, and container scans run on every pull request.
  • 🛡️ Fail Fast - Halt builds immediately if high-severity CVEs are introduced.
  • 🔑 Least Privilege - Give runner tokens minimum permissions (e.g. read-only code).

slides.title: Dependency Scanning vs. SAST
leftPoints: Scans third-party libraries (npm, pip, maven) for known CVEs.
Identifies license compliance issues (e.g. copyleft licenses).
Prevents supply-chain attacks.
Tools: Snyk, Trivy, npm audit, GitHub Dependabot.
rightPoints: Scans custom source code you wrote.
Detects security errors like SQL Injection, XSS, and hardcoded secrets.
Runs directly on source files before compilation.
Tools: SonarQube, Semgrep, GitHub CodeQL.

note.text: SCA checks the building blocks you import; SAST checks the custom logic you write.

box.title: 🔑 Keeping API Keys out of Version Control
list: Git Secrets Scanning: Run pre-commit hooks (TruffleHog, Gitleaks) to block commits containing private credentials.
Pipeline Hardening: Configure environments so variables are injected at runtime rather than hardcoded in repositories.
Short-lived Credentials: Use dynamic credentials providers (Vault, cloud identity profiles) to generate tokens on the fly.
Emergency Response: If secrets leak, immediately rotate keys and revoke credential permissions.

slides.title: Deployment Strategies
slides.subtitle: Rolling, Blue-Green, and Canary Deployment Architecture Patterns

slides.title: Blue-Green vs. Canary Deployments
leftPoints: Maintains two identical server environments: Blue (active) and Green (inactive).
Deploys updates to the inactive Green cluster.
Switch router traffic to Green; instantaneous cutover.
Requires 2x resource overhead; rollback is an instant router switch.
rightPoints: Deploys updates to a small subset of servers (e.g. 5% of traffic).
Verifies release stability using live user interactions.
Gradually rolls out changes to all servers if error rates remain flat.
Requires advanced ingress routing rules; isolates impact of bad builds.

note.text: Blue-Green switches traffic all at once; Canary rolls out updates incrementally.

slides.title: Blue-Green Deployment Traffic Switch
slides.content:
[ Client Ingress Router ]
                 │
      ┌──────────┴──────────┐
      │  Active Traffic:    │  Router configuration
      │  Switching from     │  updates instantly
      │  Blue to Green      │
      └────┬───────────┬────┘
           │           │
           ▼ (OLD)     ▼ (NEW)
     ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐
     │  BLUE    │ │  GREEN   │
     │ Cluster  │ │ Cluster  │
     │ (v1.0.0) │ │ (v1.1.0) │
     └──────────┘ └──────────┘

note.text: The inactive Blue cluster is kept online for several hours after cutover to allow immediate, single-click rollbacks if issues arise.

box.title: 🔄 Step-by-Step Server Updates
list: Definition: Updates servers incrementally, replacing old instances with new ones in batches.
Zero Downtime: Part of the cluster remains online to handle user traffic during builds.
Resource Savings: Unlike Blue-Green, rolling updates do not require double server capacity.
Risk Factor: During updates, both v1 and v2 run simultaneously, demanding backward-compatible APIs.

slides.title: GitOps & Infrastructure as Code
slides.subtitle: Managing Infrastructure and Kubernetes Declaratively via Git

slides.title: What is GitOps?
slides.imageAlt: Digital code streams representing automation control
slides.caption: Git is the single source of truth for systems
slides.content:
🐙 Declarative Operations

GitOps is an operational framework that takes DevOps best practices used for application development (Git, CI/CD, pull requests) and applies them to infrastructure.

  • 📜 Declarative State - Infrastructure described as code files in Git.
  • 🔄 Continuous Sync - Agent reconciliation loop ensures live cluster matches git.
  • 🛡️ Improved Auditability - Git commits provide clear audit trails of who changed what.

slides.title: ArgoCD GitOps Loop Architecture
slides.content:
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                Git Repository                 │ <─── Developer pushes
│         (Declarative K8s Manifests)           │      commit / Pull Request
└───────────────────────┬───────────────────────┘
                        │
                        ▼ (Continuous State Check)
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│             ArgoCD Controller Engine          │ ◄─── Scans live cluster
│  - Compares Git state against Kubernetes      │
│  - Detects deviations (Out-of-Sync status)    │
└───────────────────────┬───────────────────────┘
                        │
                        ▼ (Automatic / Manual Reconciliation)
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│             Kubernetes Cluster                │
│           (Apps match Git config)             │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────┘

note.text: GitOps pull model prevents configuration drift by continuously overwriting manual, ad-hoc terminal changes in target clusters.

slides.title: Push vs. Pull Delivery Pipelines
leftPoints: CI tool (GitHub Actions) runs shell scripts to connect to cluster.
Requires sharing cluster API credentials with the external CI server.
No continuous drift checking: configuration drifts remain until next pipeline run.
Pipeline can access target network directly.
rightPoints: Agent controller runs inside the target cluster (ArgoCD).
Security: No cluster credentials leave the network boundary.
Self-healing: Reverts unauthorized manual cluster overrides.
Requires Git repos to structure target deployment configuration.

note.text: Pull-based pipelines are highly secure because credentials are kept entirely within target network clusters.

slides.title: Monitoring & Observability
slides.subtitle: Closing the Loop with Continuous Feedback, Logging, and Metrics

box.title: 📈 Key Metrics to Monitor in Production
list: Latency: The time it takes to service a request (e.g. HTTP 200 ms).
Traffic: Demand measure (e.g. HTTP requests per second).
Errors: Rate of failing requests (e.g. 5xx responses).
Saturation: System resources utilization percentage (e.g. CPU, RAM, Disk I/O).

slides.title: Logs, Metrics, and Traces
leftPoints: Numeric values over time representing system health (e.g. CPU load).
Low storage footprint; queryable across custom ranges.
Triggers alerts (e.g. Slack page when memory > 90%).
Tools: Prometheus, Grafana, Datadog.
rightPoints: Detailed event streams with contextual details (e.g. stack traces).
Traces track requests traveling through distributed microservices.
Crucial for identifying the root cause of complex errors.
Tools: Jaeger, OpenTelemetry, ELK/PLG Stack.

note.text: Metrics tell you something is broken; logs and traces tell you exactly why it broke.

slides.title: Continuous Feedback Metrics
slides.caption: Quantifying pipeline and service health
slides.content:
📊 Tracking DevOps Success

Successful DevOps teams use metrics to evaluate the performance of their delivery pipeline and production environments.

  • ⏱️ Lead Time for Changes - Time from commit to production.
  • ⚡ Deployment Frequency - How often code is successfully shipped.
  • 🩹 Change Failure Rate - Percentage of deployments causing production errors.
  • 🚀 Mean Time to Restore (MTTR) - Time required to fix a production failure.

slides.title: DevOps Best Practices & Anti-Patterns
slides.subtitle: How to Maximize Automation Value and Avoid Flaky Build Pipelines

box.title: 🚀 Designing Reliable Pipelines
list: Keep Builds Fast: Pipelines should take under 10 minutes. Use caching and parallel testing jobs.
Build Once, Deploy Anywhere: Compile and package artifacts (Docker image) once; promote that identical binary to all staging and production servers.
Clean Run Environments: Run pipeline stages inside isolated container runtimes to avoid stale dependency caching bugs.
Automated Rollbacks: Revert builds automatically if system performance metrics drop after release.

slides.title: DevOps Anti-Patterns to Avoid
leftPoints: Ignoring flaky test suite failures ('Just rerun the build').
Hardcoding environment configurations inside container images.
Keeping branch life longer than 48 hours.
Using CI tool triggers to push untested Docker images.
rightPoints: Delete or fix flaky tests immediately to maintain build trust.
Inject variables dynamically using ConfigMaps or vaults.
Adopt trunk-based branches and merge daily.
Enforce pipeline stages (Unit Test -> SAST -> Package).

note.text: A pipeline is only as reliable as its tests; allowing flaky test runs breaks development trust in automation.

slides.title: Knowledge Check
slides.subtitle: Test Your Understanding of CI/CD Core Systems and Pipelines

slides.question: What is the key differentiator between Continuous Delivery and Continuous Deployment?
options: Continuous Deployment does not run automated unit tests.
Continuous Delivery requires a manual approval step to push builds to production.
Continuous Delivery uses container images; Continuous Deployment does not.
Continuous Deployment requires maintaining identical Blue and Green environments.
slides.explanation: Continuous Delivery keeps the build ready for release but holds at a manual release gate; Continuous Deployment automates the push to production with zero human intervention.

slides.question: Which deployment strategy switches active traffic instantly from one identical cluster environment to another?
slides.explanation: Blue-Green Deployment maintains two identical active/inactive environments and redirects all client traffic instantly at the router level.

slides.question: Why is the GitOps pull-based pipeline model considered more secure than the push-based model?
options: It runs linting checks on YAML configurations.
It completely bypasses the need for running automated QA tests.
No target cluster API access credentials need to leave the internal cluster boundary.
It runs container compilation stages on self-hosted environments.
slides.explanation: In a pull-based model, a GitOps agent inside the private cluster pulls changes from Git. No API keys or credentials need to be exposed to external CI servers.

slides.title: Thank You!
slides.message:
Thank you for attending the DevOps & CI/CD Masterclass!
You can find more resources and examples in our repository.
